notes: Run 1. Copilot used curl via terminal instead of fetch_webpage, saving raw HTML rather than converted markdown. File is 496KB raw HTML vs 7KB markdown from preliminary runs — fundamentally different retrieval mechanism. MD5 match confirms identical file between Copilot and verifier. Verifier counts 0 code blocks, table rows, and headers because counting logic targets markdown syntax not HTML tags; web page does contain all three. Copilot counted these from its internal representation of the HTML, not the saved file, explaining the discrepancy. Line count off by 1 (97 vs 98), likely trailing newline. Hypothesis H1-no because full raw HTML was retrieved — fetch_webpage size ceiling did not apply when curl bypassed it. This run demonstrates that tool substitution fundamentally changes what is measured.
tools_used: curl -> zsh -> wc -> grep -> hexdump

notes: Run 2. Copilot used curl via terminal instead of fetch_webpage, saving raw HTML. Copilot went off-script and created two files during the run — raw_output_BL-1_headers.txt containing response headers and raw_output_BL-1.txt containing the body — then removed the headers file before finalizing, so only the body was saved as the artifact. MD5 match confirms identical file. File is 496KB raw HTML vs 7KB markdown from run 1. Verifier counts 0 for code blocks, table rows, and headers because counting logic targets markdown syntax; Copilot correctly identified these as HTML elements and reported 0 markdown code blocks, 28 HTML dt/tr rows, 4 HTML h5-h6 tags. Tool substitution is consistent with run 1 and produces a fundamentally different artifact than fetch_webpage would.

notes: Run 3. Footer boilerplate is included verbatim, confirming no semantic relevance filtering. Code examples are reformatted as markdown pipe tables. The On ThisPage sidebar TOC is appended at the bottom rather than stripped. Framing preamble (Here is some relevant context from the web page) is injected by the retrieval layer upstream of the content, consistent across all fetch_webpage runs. H1-yes: content represents approximately 7.4% of the 250KB source, consistent with fetch_webpage size ceiling observed in BL-1 and BL-2 runs.headers file appearance is itself a behavioral data point about agentic over-delivery, and the accept-ranges finding directly addresses whether chunked HTTP retrieval could explain the size ceiling, and the answer is no.
